brand.name: FCEI
brand.positioning: Finnish-inspired, EDUFI-aligned and FINEEC-benchmarked Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework

courses.title: Foundations of Finnish-Inspired Education
courses.description: Finnish-Education Values: Equity, Trust, Wellbeing and Joy of Learning is part of the FCEI Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. The course translates Finnish-inspired principles into local, evidence-informed practice through 6 modules. EDUFI alignment emphasises equity, participation, wellbeing, support, meaningful learning and local curriculum adaptation. FINEEC benchmarking emphasises enhancement-led self-evaluation, quality culture, trust and evidence-based improvement. Participants complete practical tasks, upload evidence and produce reusable professional artefacts for their classroom, team, institution, TVET context or consultancy setting.
courses.audience: Teachers, educators, leaders and implementation teams

modules.title: Principle in Action: Values Become Visible
modules.courseTitle: Finnish-Education Values: Equity, Trust, Wellbeing and Joy of Learning
modules.aim: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Ethical Finnish-Inspired Positioning into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. EDUFI alignment: local curriculum and equality. FINEEC benchmarking: trust-based improvement. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real classroom, team or institutional problem without blame, applies Values-to-Practice Audit, collects evidence such as routine observation, learner access evidence, staff reflection, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Ethical Finnish-Inspired Positioning implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.
modules.principle: Principle in Action: Values Become Visible is visible when the principle can be seen in learner experience, educator or leader behaviour, and an artefact that proves what changed.
modules.problem: A school says it values principle in action: values become visible, but the value is not visible in classroom routines, leadership decisions or learner evidence.
modules.actionTask: In your own context, complete a values-to-evidence mini-audit and make one value visible through one learner-facing routine. Keep the action small enough to complete, review and improve without requiring whole-school rollout first.
modules.evidenceUpload: Upload values-to-evidence map + 100-word local adaptation note. Add date, context, anonymisation note where learner data is used, and a 100-word explanation of what the evidence shows.
reflectionPrompts: What local problem did you diagnose and why is it important?
Which Finnish-aligned principle guided your action?
What evidence did you collect and what does it honestly show?
What should be kept, adapted, repeated, scaled carefully or stopped?

keyTerms.definition: define this in relation to the participant's local practice and evidence.
define this in relation to the participant's local practice and evidence.

objectives: Define the key Finnish-aligned principle behind Principle in Action: Values Become Visible.
Diagnose the local problem using observable evidence instead of blame.
Apply the Values-to-Evidence Map to one manageable action.
Produce and upload the required artefact: values-to-evidence map + 100-word local adaptation note.
Use the seven-step Transferability Filter to decide whether to keep, adapt, scale or stop the action.
outcomes: Participants can explain principle in action: values become visible in plain professional language.
Participants can identify which sphere owns the change: classroom, management, institution, workplace or consultancy.
Participants can implement one low-risk trial or diagnostic step.
Participants can collect evidence and interpret it honestly.
Participants can record a next-step improvement decision suitable for their local context.

quiz.question: What is the most responsible first move when applying Principle in Action: Values Become Visible in a new local context?
options: Copy the Finnish practice exactly without change.
Test one manageable action, collect evidence and adapt before scaling.
Launch a whole-school reform immediately.
Wait until every condition is perfect before trying anything.
quiz.rationale: The best answer protects learners and staff through a bounded evidence-first trial.

quiz.question: Which evidence best proves that Principle in Action: Values Become Visible changed practice?
options: Attendance or page views only.
The required artefact is completed and linked to a reflection: values-to-evidence map + 100-word local adaptation note.
A general statement that participants enjoyed the topic.
A certificate issued before evidence is uploaded.
quiz.rationale: FCEI completion must show implementation, evidence and reflection, not participation only.

quiz.question: What should be avoided when implementing Principle in Action: Values Become Visible?
options: Treating Finnish values as slogans instead of observable learner, teacher and leadership behaviours.
Using local evidence before scaling.
Keeping the same learning aim while adapting access or process.
Writing the change into a local plan or routine.
quiz.rationale: This distractor is the exact misconception the module is designed to correct.

FINEEC benchmarking: enhancement-led self-evaluation, quality culture and evidence-informed improvement. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real classroom, team or institutional problem without blame, applies Learner Voice Planner, collects evidence such as learner reflection, student choice record, collaborative task evidence, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Learner Voice and Choice implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.

modules.courseTitle: Formative Assessment, Feedback and Self-Assessment
modules.aim: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Self-Assessment and Success Criteria into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. EDUFI alignment: continuous assessment during studies and regular feedback. FINEEC benchmarking: enhancement-led self-evaluation, quality culture and evidence-informed improvement. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real classroom, team or institutional problem without blame, applies Formative Evidence Map, collects evidence such as feedback samples, self-assessment record, mini-checkpoint evidence, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Self-Assessment and Success Criteria implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.
modules.principle: Learner Self-Assessment Tools is visible when the principle can be seen in learner experience, educator or leader behaviour, and an artefact that proves what changed.
modules.problem: Teachers mark work after the lesson, but learner self-assessment tools arrives too late to change teaching or help learners improve the same task.
modules.actionTask: In your own context, insert one evidence pause and one improvement moment into an existing lesson. Keep the action small enough to complete, review and improve without requiring whole-school rollout first.
modules.evidenceUpload: Upload success criteria + before/after learner work or exit-ticket summary. Add date, context, anonymisation note where learner data is used, and a 100-word explanation of what the evidence shows.
reflectionPrompts: What local problem did you diagnose and why is it important?
Which Finnish-aligned principle guided your action?
What evidence did you collect and what does it honestly show?
What should be kept, adapted, repeated, scaled carefully or stopped?

objectives: Define the key Finnish-aligned principle behind Learner Self-Assessment Tools.
Diagnose the local problem using observable evidence instead of blame.
Apply the Evidence-to-Feedback Loop to one manageable action.
Produce and upload the required artefact: success criteria + before/after learner work or exit-ticket summary.
Use the seven-step Transferability Filter to decide whether to keep, adapt, scale or stop the action.
outcomes: Participants can explain learner self-assessment tools in plain professional language.
Participants can identify which sphere owns the change: classroom, management, institution, workplace or consultancy.
Participants can implement one low-risk trial or diagnostic step.
Participants can collect evidence and interpret it honestly.
Participants can record a next-step improvement decision suitable for their local context.

resources.title: C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Concept Brief and Finnish Anchor
C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Scenario Diagnosis Worksheet
C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Evidence-to-Feedback Loop Template
C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Evidence Upload Template: success criteria + before/after learner work or exit-ticket summary
C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Transferability Filter Reflection Sheet
C4.3 Learner Self-Assessment Tools - Portfolio Checklist and Completion Rubric

quiz.question: What is the most responsible first move when applying Learner Self-Assessment Tools in a new local context?
options: Copy the Finnish practice exactly without change.
Test one manageable action, collect evidence and adapt before scaling.
Launch a whole-school reform immediately.
Wait until every condition is perfect before trying anything.
quiz.rationale: The best answer protects learners and staff through a bounded evidence-first trial.

quiz.question: Which evidence best proves that Learner Self-Assessment Tools changed practice?
options: Attendance or page views only.
The required artefact is completed and linked to a reflection: success criteria + before/after learner work or exit-ticket summary.
A general statement that participants enjoyed the topic.
A certificate issued before evidence is uploaded.
quiz.rationale: FCEI completion must show implementation, evidence and reflection, not participation only.

quiz.question: What should be avoided when implementing Learner Self-Assessment Tools?
options: Adding more marks, grades or comments without giving learners time to improve.
Using local evidence before scaling.
Keeping the same learning aim while adapting access or process.
Writing the change into a local plan or routine.
quiz.rationale: This distractor is the exact misconception the module is designed to correct.

modules.aim: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Observable Learner Need into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. EDUFI alignment: support to learning, positive differentiation and pupil welfare. FINEEC benchmarking: enhancement-led self-evaluation, quality culture and evidence-informed improvement. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real classroom, team or institutional problem without blame, applies Early Support Observation Form, collects evidence such as learner need note, support route plan, parent/team communication record, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Observable Learner Need implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.

modules.evidenceUpload: Upload support trigger note + revised access task + learner response review. Add date, context, anonymisation note where learner data is used, and a 100-word explanation of what the evidence shows.

keyTerms.definition: A structured professional improvement process where a teacher or education team identifies a real practice issue, collects evidence, tests a manageable action and makes a justified improvement decision.
The practical starting evidence collected before a change is introduced, such as learner work, observation notes, learner voice, assessment samples or participation records.
A safe, limited implementation step used to test one improvement before scaling it to a full class, team or institution.
Learners' spoken, written or demonstrated feedback about access, confidence, participation, challenge, progress and support.
The educator's evidence-informed decision-making about learning, support, feedback, assessment and next steps.
A development-focused evaluation approach that emphasises participation, trust, learning, improvement and the identification of strengths as well as development targets.
A structured collection of artefacts showing the inquiry question, baseline, action, evidence, reflection and improvement decision.
The FCEI cycle of identifying a principle, diagnosing the problem, choosing a tool, collecting evidence, reflecting and deciding the next improvement step.

modules.shortExplanation: This module helps participants define a focused, ethical and evidence-informed teacher inquiry question. It moves the participant from a general concern to a clear problem of practice that can be investigated through a small, manageable improvement cycle.

Final deliverable: a completed Baseline Evidence Collection implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.

quiz.question: What is the most responsible first move when applying Blended Learning With Pedagogical Purpose in a new local context?
options: Copy the Finnish practice exactly without change.
Test one manageable action, collect evidence and adapt before scaling.
Launch a whole-school reform immediately.
Wait until every condition is perfect before trying anything.
quiz.rationale: The best answer protects learners and staff through a bounded evidence-first trial.

quiz.question: Which evidence best proves that Blended Learning With Pedagogical Purpose changed practice?
options: Attendance or page views only.
The required artefact is completed and linked to a reflection: LMS workflow audit + accessibility/completion evidence.
A general statement that participants enjoyed the topic.
A certificate issued before evidence is uploaded.
quiz.rationale: FCEI completion must show implementation, evidence and reflection, not participation only.

quiz.question: What should be avoided when implementing Blended Learning With Pedagogical Purpose?
options: Counting platform completion without implementation evidence, reflection or feedback.
Using local evidence before scaling.
Keeping the same learning aim while adapting access or process.
Writing the change into a local plan or routine.
quiz.rationale: This distractor is the exact misconception the module is designed to correct.

modules.shortExplanation: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Blended Learning Course Sequence into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. EDUFI alignment: digital/ICT competence. FINEEC benchmarking: planned and monitored quality management. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real classroom, team or institutional problem without blame, applies Blended Delivery Blueprint, collects evidence such as module page checklist, evidence upload workflow, certificate rule test record, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Blended Learning Course Sequence implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.

modules.courseTitle: TVET Competence-Based Learning and Workplace Evidence
modules.aim: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Rewrite TVET Topics as Observable Work Tasks into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. objectives: Define the key Finnish-aligned principle behind Commercial QA, Reporting and Renewal Cycle.
Diagnose the local problem using observable evidence instead of blame.
Apply the Consultancy Diagnosis and Implementation Roadmap to one manageable action.
Produce and upload the required artefact: client diagnosis + workshop plan + implementation roadmap + QA report.
Use the seven-step Transferability Filter to decide whether to keep, adapt, scale or stop the action.
outcomes: Participants can explain commercial qa, reporting and renewal cycle in plain professional language.
Participants can identify which sphere owns the change: classroom, management, institution, workplace or consultancy.
Participants can implement one low-risk trial or diagnostic step.
Participants can collect evidence and interpret it honestly.
Participants can record a next-step improvement decision suitable for their local context.

quiz.question: What is the most responsible first move when applying Commercial QA, Reporting and Renewal Cycle in a new local context?
options: Copy the Finnish practice exactly without change.
Test one manageable action, collect evidence and adapt before scaling.
Launch a whole-school reform immediately.
Wait until every condition is perfect before trying anything.
quiz.rationale: The best answer protects learners and staff through a bounded evidence-first trial.

quiz.question: Which evidence best proves that Commercial QA, Reporting and Renewal Cycle changed practice?
options: Attendance or page views only.
The required artefact is completed and linked to a reflection: client diagnosis + workshop plan + implementation roadmap + QA report.
A general statement that participants enjoyed the topic.
A certificate issued before evidence is uploaded.
quiz.rationale: FCEI completion must show implementation, evidence and reflection, not participation only.

quiz.question: What should be avoided when implementing Commercial QA, Reporting and Renewal Cycle?
options: Selling workshops without diagnosis, evidence, implementation support or follow-up review.
Using local evidence before scaling.
Keeping the same learning aim while adapting access or process.
Writing the change into a local plan or routine.
quiz.rationale: This distractor is the exact misconception the module is designed to correct.

modules.shortExplanation: EDUFI/FINEEC framing: This FCEI module turns Commercial Consultancy Handoff, Reporting and Renewal into a practical, evidence-led task within the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework. EDUFI alignment: local curriculum logic. FINEEC benchmarking: quality management and enhancement-led evaluation. In FCEI practice, the participant is not only reading a course page; they are building a reusable professional artefact. The module defines the principle, names the real leadership or institutional problem without blame, applies Institutional Needs Analysis, collects evidence such as needs analysis data, workshop agendas, implementation plans, coaching notes, institutional reports and renewal proposals, completed task sheet, template output, reflection or demonstration record, and uses the improvement cycle to decide the next step. Final deliverable: a completed Commercial Consultancy Handoff, Reporting and Renewal implementation pack containing a problem statement, practical tool selection, implementation steps, evidence record, reflection and improvement decision.

items.title: Terms and Conditions
items.desc: Account creation, course access, intellectual property, payment terms, certificates, acceptable use, and liability limitations.
body: 1. Acceptance of Terms: By accessing or using the FCEI platform, you agree to these Terms and Conditions. If you do not agree, please do not use the platform.

2. Account Registration: Users must provide accurate information when creating an account. You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your login credentials and for all activities under your account.

3. Course Access: Upon successful payment, you will receive access to the purchased course materials. Access is granted on a per-user, non-transferable basis. Course content remains available for 12 months from the date of purchase unless otherwise stated.

4. Intellectual Property: All course materials, including videos, text, quizzes, rubrics, and frameworks, are the intellectual property of the Finland Creative Education Institute. You may not reproduce, distribute, modify, or create derivative works without prior written consent.
5. Payment Terms: All prices are displayed in USD. Payment is processed securely through Stripe. Prices may vary by geographic region. Full payment is required before course access is granted.

6. Certificates: Upon successful completion of all course modules, including quizzes, evidence uploads, and reflections, a Capstone Certificate will be issued. Certificates are for professional development purposes and do not constitute formal teaching qualifications.

7. Acceptable Use: You agree to use the platform for lawful educational purposes only. Any misuse, including sharing login credentials, downloading content for redistribution, or disrupting the platform, may result in account termination.

8. Limitation of Liability: FCEI provides educational content on an as-is basis. We do not guarantee specific career outcomes or professional recognition. Our liability is limited to the amount paid for the course.
9. Modifications: FCEI reserves the right to update these terms at any time. Continued use of the platform after changes constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.
10. Governing Law: These terms are governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Finland. Any disputes shall be resolved in the courts of Helsinki, Finland.

items.title: Privacy Policy
items.desc: What data we collect, how it is stored and used, third-party processors, your rights, and GDPR compliance.
body: 1. Data Controller: The Finland Creative Education Institute (FCEI) is the data controller for personal data collected through this platform.

2. Data We Collect: We collect your name, email address, and payment information when you register and purchase courses. We also collect learning progress data, quiz responses, evidence uploads, and certificate records as part of the learning experience.

3. How We Use Your Data: Your data is used to provide course access, track learning progress, issue certificates, process payments, and communicate with you about your learning. We may use anonymised, aggregated data for platform improvement.

4. Third-Party Processors: We use Stripe for payment processing. Stripe processes your card details securely under their own privacy policy. We use Vimeo for video hosting. No personal data is shared with Vimeo beyond standard video playback.
5. Data Storage: Your data is stored on secure servers within the European Economic Area (EEA). We implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect your data.

6. Data Retention: Account and learning data is retained for the duration of your account plus 3 years. Payment records are retained for 7 years as required by financial regulations. You may request deletion of your account at any time.

7. Your Rights (GDPR): You have the right to access, rectify, erase, restrict processing, data portability, and object to processing of your personal data. To exercise these rights, contact us using the details provided in the Complaints and Contact Policy.
8. Cookies: Please refer to our Cookie Policy for details on how we use cookies and similar technologies.
9. Updates: This privacy policy may be updated periodically. The last updated date is shown at the top of this page.

items.title: Cookie Policy
items.desc: Types of cookies and local storage used, consent mechanism, how to manage preferences, and third-party analytics.
body: 1. What Are Cookies: Cookies are small text files stored on your device when you visit a website. They help the website function properly and provide information to the site owners.
2. Essential Cookies: We use essential cookies to maintain your login session and remember your preferences. These are strictly necessary for the platform to function and cannot be disabled.
3. Analytics Cookies: We may use analytics tools to understand how visitors use our platform. These cookies collect anonymised information about page views, navigation patterns, and feature usage.
4. No Advertising Cookies: FCEI does not use advertising or tracking cookies. We do not share your browsing data with advertisers or third-party marketing services.
5. Local Storage: We use browser local storage to save your learning progress, theme preferences, and session data. This data remains on your device and is not transmitted to external servers.
6. Managing Cookies: You can manage cookies through your browser settings. Disabling essential cookies may prevent you from logging in or accessing course content.

7. Third-Party Cookies: Vimeo (video hosting) and Stripe (payment processing) may set their own cookies when you interact with embedded videos or the checkout process. These are governed by their respective cookie policies.

items.title: Refund and Cancellation Policy
items.desc: 14-day cooling-off period, digital product terms, subscription cancellations, school licence refunds, and Stripe processing.
body: 1. Cooling-Off Period: You have 14 days from the date of purchase to request a full refund, provided you have not accessed more than 20% of the course content (i.e., no more than 1 module out of 6).

2. Digital Product Terms: As digital educational content, courses are delivered immediately upon purchase. By proceeding with a purchase, you acknowledge that access to digital content begins immediately.

3. Refund Process: To request a refund, contact us through the details provided in the Complaints and Contact Policy. Refunds are processed within 14 business days via the original payment method (Stripe).
4. Partial Refunds: If you have accessed more than 20% of a course but less than 50%, a partial refund of 50% of the purchase price may be granted at our discretion.
5. No Refund After Completion: Courses that have been fully completed or for which a certificate has been issued are not eligible for a refund.
6. Institutional Licences: Refunds for institutional or group licences are handled on a case-by-case basis. Please contact us to discuss.
7. Stripe Processing: All payments and refunds are processed through Stripe. FCEI does not store your card details. Refund processing times may vary depending on your bank.

items.title: Accessibility Statement
items.desc: WCAG compliance, assistive technology support, accessibility features, and continuous improvement commitment.
body: 1. Our Commitment: FCEI is committed to ensuring that our platform is accessible to all users, including those with disabilities. We aim to conform to WCAG 2.1 Level AA standards.

2. Accessibility Features: The platform supports keyboard navigation, screen readers, and high-contrast display. Course content is presented in clear, readable typography with sufficient colour contrast.
3. Video Content: Course videos include clear audio narration. We are working towards providing captions and transcripts for all video content.
4. Responsive Design: The platform is designed to work across devices, including desktops, tablets, and mobile phones, ensuring access regardless of the device used.
5. Assistive Technology: We test our platform with common assistive technologies, including screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver) and keyboard-only navigation.

6. Continuous Improvement: We regularly review and improve the accessibility of our platform. If you encounter any accessibility barriers, please contact us and we will work to resolve the issue promptly.
7. Feedback: We welcome feedback on the accessibility of our platform. Please contact us through the Complaints and Contact Policy section.

items.title: AI Content and Avatar Disclosure
items.desc: Use of AI tools in content creation, videos, human oversight, and transparency commitment.
body: 1. Transparency Commitment: FCEI is committed to transparency regarding the use of artificial intelligence in our educational content and platform operations.

2. AI-Generated Video Presenters: Some course videos feature AI-generated presenters (avatars) created using HeyGen technology. These avatars deliver course content based on scripts developed and reviewed by FCEI education specialists.

3. Content Development: AI tools may be used to assist in drafting, structuring, or refining course materials. All AI-assisted content undergoes human review by qualified educators before publication.

4. Human Oversight: Every piece of content on this platform, whether AI-assisted or not, is reviewed, validated, and approved by FCEI's team of education professionals. The pedagogical framework, learning outcomes, and assessment criteria are designed by human experts.
5. Quality Assurance: We maintain rigorous quality standards regardless of the tools used in content creation. AI is used as a productivity tool, not as a replacement for educational expertise.

6. No AI in Assessment: Quiz questions, rubrics, evidence evaluation criteria, and certificate issuance decisions are designed and managed by human educators. AI is not used to grade or evaluate learner submissions.
7. Updates: As AI technology evolves, we will update this disclosure to reflect any changes in how AI tools are used within the platform.

items.title: Accreditation and Certification
items.desc: Certificate Scope, Finnish-Education Positioning, Non-Affiliation unless stated, and Training Recognition
body: The Finland Creative Education Institute (FCEI) delivers structured Finnish-Education Capstone Certification Pathways and comprehensive institutional improvement frameworks. We provide EDUFI and FINEEC-integrated professional training, core curriculum standards, institutional architecture, and expert consultancy.

A Capstone Certification is widely used across Finnish teacher education, university programmes, and professional development pathways. It serves as a formal validation of a participant’s engagement, successful completion of training requirements, and—most importantly—their demonstrated ability to apply learning in authentic, practical contexts. The exact emphasis varies by programme, ranging from recognition of participation to evidence-based demonstration of professional competence.

Institutional & Regulatory Notice: Within the Finnish education ecosystem, official teaching qualifications are structurally embedded within academic degrees awarded exclusively by accredited universities, while the recognition of foreign qualifications is managed solely by the Finnish National Agency for Education (EDUFI).

Accordingly, Capstone Certificates serve as advanced professional development credentials, documenting high-level training and demonstrated competence aligned with core Finnish education principles. They represent rigorous, practice-based mastery but do not constitute a formal teaching licence, state-issued qualification, or regulated academic credit, unless explicitly specified for a particular programme track.

Recognition of Capstone Certificates varies by employer, institution, ministry, or local regulator; participants are responsible for confirming their regional compliance requirements. Ultimately, the market value of Capstone certificate is driven and strengthened by the participant’s active engagement, evidence submission, and demonstrated application of institutional improvement practices.

items.title: Acceptable Use Policy
items.desc: Copyright protection, permitted use, prohibited conduct, content sharing restrictions, and enforcement.
body: 1. Permitted Use: The FCEI platform is provided for personal and professional educational development. You may access course materials, complete assessments, and download your own certificates.

2. Copyright Protection: All content on this platform is protected by copyright. You may not copy, reproduce, distribute, or share course materials, including videos, quizzes, rubrics, and text content, without prior written permission from FCEI.
3. Account Sharing: Each account is for individual use only. Sharing login credentials with others is strictly prohibited and may result in account termination without refund.

4. Prohibited Conduct: You may not attempt to access other users' accounts or data, reverse-engineer or scrape the platform, upload malicious content, use the platform for any unlawful purpose, or interfere with the platform's normal operation.

5. Content Sharing: You may share your certificate and course completion status publicly. However, sharing specific course content, quiz answers, or evidence frameworks outside the platform is prohibited.
6. Enforcement: Violations of this policy may result in temporary suspension, permanent account termination, or legal action, depending on the severity of the violation.

items.title: Data Processing Addendum
items.desc: Institutional data processing agreements, GDPR compliance for schools, sub-processors, and data handling.
body: 1. Purpose: This Data Processing Addendum (DPA) applies to institutional customers who enrol groups of learners and governs how FCEI processes personal data on behalf of the institution.

2. Roles: For institutional licences, the subscribing institution acts as the Data Controller, and FCEI acts as the Data Processor. FCEI processes learner data only as instructed by the institution and in accordance with applicable data protection laws.

3. GDPR Compliance: FCEI processes all data in compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). We implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.

4. Sub-Processors: FCEI uses the following sub-processors: Stripe (payment processing, EU/US), Vimeo (video hosting, US with EU data protections). Institutions will be notified of any changes to sub-processors.
5. Data Location: All learner data is stored within the European Economic Area (EEA). Data may be accessed from outside the EEA only with appropriate safeguards in place.
6. Data Breach: In the event of a personal data breach, FCEI will notify the institution within 72 hours and provide all necessary information to comply with breach notification requirements.
7. Termination: Upon termination of the institutional agreement, FCEI will delete or return all personal data within 30 days, unless retention is required by law.

items.title: Safeguarding and Children's Data
items.desc: Child protection, pseudonymised student data, DBS responsibilities, professional boundaries, and school obligations.
body: 1. Child Protection: FCEI takes the safeguarding of children and vulnerable adults seriously. While our courses are primarily designed for adult educators, some institutional programmes may involve data relating to minors.

2. Pseudonymised Student Data: When case studies or school improvement data is referenced in evidence submissions, all student data must be pseudonymised. No identifiable information about children should be uploaded to the platform.
3. Age Restrictions: The FCEI platform is intended for users aged 18 and over. Individuals under 18 may only use the platform under the supervision of their institution with appropriate consent.

4. DBS and Vetting: FCEI does not manage DBS (Disclosure and Barring Service) checks or equivalent vetting processes. It is the responsibility of the employing institution to ensure that their staff have appropriate clearances.

5. Reporting Concerns: If you encounter any content or behaviour on the platform that raises safeguarding concerns, please contact us immediately using the details in the Complaints and Contact Policy.
6. Professional Standards: All FCEI consultants, trainers, and facilitators are expected to adhere to professional safeguarding standards as outlined in our Consultant and Trainer Terms.

items.title: Complaints and Contact Policy
items.desc: How to raise concerns, response timelines, escalation process, and contact information.
body: 1. Raising a Concern: If you have a complaint or concern about the FCEI platform, courses, or services, please contact us at: info@fcei.eu. We take all feedback seriously and aim to resolve issues promptly.
2. Response Timeline: We will acknowledge your complaint within 2 business days and aim to provide a full response within 10 business days.
3. Escalation: If you are not satisfied with the initial response, you may request escalation to FCEI senior management. Escalated complaints will be reviewed within 15 business days.

4. Data Protection Queries: For questions about how your data is processed, or to exercise your GDPR rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability), please contact our Data Protection contact at: info@fcei.eu.
5. External Bodies: If you feel your complaint has not been adequately resolved, you may contact the Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman (tietosuoja.fi) for data protection matters.
6. Feedback: We welcome constructive feedback at any time. Your input helps us improve the platform and learning experience for all users.

items.title: Consultant and Trainer Terms
items.desc: Terms for FCEI-affiliated trainers, consultants, facilitators, and workshop delivery partners.
body: 1. Scope: These terms apply to all individuals engaged by FCEI as consultants, trainers, facilitators, or workshop deliverers, whether on a contracted or freelance basis.

2. Professional Conduct: All FCEI-affiliated trainers must maintain the highest standards of professional conduct, including respect for diversity, safeguarding awareness, and adherence to FCEI's educational values.

3. Intellectual Property: Materials developed for or on behalf of FCEI remain the intellectual property of FCEI. Trainers may not use FCEI materials for non-FCEI engagements without prior written consent.

4. Confidentiality: Trainers must maintain the confidentiality of institutional client information, learner data, and any proprietary FCEI methodologies or frameworks they are exposed to during their engagement.
5. Quality Standards: All training delivered under the FCEI brand must align with the Finnish Teacher Training Development Framework and meet the quality standards set out by FCEI.

6. Termination: Either party may terminate the engagement with 30 days' written notice. FCEI reserves the right to immediately terminate the engagement in cases of gross misconduct, safeguarding concerns, or breach of these terms.
7. Liability: Trainers are responsible for their own tax obligations, insurance, and professional indemnity. FCEI does not assume employer liability for contracted trainers.

legal.questionsBody: If you have questions about any of our legal or compliance policies, or wish to exercise your data rights, please contact us via the Complaints and Contact page or use the contact form.
